dialogue.utterance: All units, ER-77 to 5501 [CITY] Road for a 26-year-old female with unconsciousness/fainting/near-fainting.
Good evening, I'm Jordan. What made you call 911 today?
I had chest pain a couple of hours ago. It felt like a panic attack.
Can you tell me your name?
My name is Ashley.
Ashley, what year and today's date are we in?
2020, today is April 25th.
Ashley, can you tell me what county we're in?
[CITY] County.
Ashley, what happened?
I had chest pain a while ago, and I was too scared not to call.
Ashley, I'll check your breathing. Can I listen to your lungs?
Breathing is even and regular. Respirations are at 18 per minute.
Your airway is clear. I don't see any signs of obstruction.
I'm going to check your pulse.
Pulse is 122 beats per minute and strong.
Respirations are 18 and even.
Blood pressure is 126/81.
Ashley, can you tell me your current orientation: person, place, time, and event?
Ashley, April 25th, [CITY] County, and I had chest pain.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15 (E4, V5, M6). Ashley is alert and oriented to all.
Oxygen saturation is 99%, which is in the normal range.
Glucose could not be measured due to high interference in the results.
Skin is pink, warm, and dry.

No neurological deficits were noted. 26. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; EMT: When was the worst time for your chest discomfort? 27. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: It began around noon today. 28. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; EMT: Where in your chest was the pain located? 29. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: Right in the middle of my chest.
Did the pain come gradually or suddenly?
It came slowly with my anxiety.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst, how severe was the pain?
Around a 2. It's not too bad, but it still worries me.
Was it sharp, pressure-like, or more like tension?
It felt more like tension, not sharp at all.
Did it radiate to other areas like your arms or back?
No, it stayed in the middle of my chest. 38. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: relevant symptoms; EMT: Any shortness of breath or other symptoms related to this?

No, I don't have shortness of breath. 40. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: relevant symptoms; EMT: Any abdominal pain or neurological issues? 41. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: relevant symptoms; Patient: No, just the chest discomfort.

We have your vitals: 122 heart rate, 126 over 81 BP, 99% oxygen saturation, and a normal respiratory rate. All within a safe range. 43. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; EMT: Any history of behavioral or psychiatric conditions? 44. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; Patient: I have bipolar disorder and anxiety.
What medications are you currently on?
I take methadone regularly.
Do you have any drug allergies? 48. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: allergies; Patient: No known drug allergies.

What did you eat or drink before the symptoms started? 50. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: last oral intake; Patient: Around lunchtime, I had some soup and water. 51. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: events leading up to the event; EMT: What were you doing the day before and today before the pain?

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: I was taking care of my baby; she's six weeks old.
Tympanic temperature is 99.1°F, slightly elevated.
Ashley is in a semi-Fowler position and appears to be resting well.
You seem anxious. Has this happened to you before?
Yes, like a panic attack from the stress of being a new mom.
We're going to place EKG leads on your chest now.
EKG leads are on, and we're monitoring.
EKG shows sinus tach with nonspecific ST and T wave abnormality.
No immediate danger noted. Ashley is alert, oriented, and has no deficits.
Let's follow Protocol 2-1 for chest pain and possible cardiac issues. No acute compromise found.
Ashley, would you like to go to a hospital if you're comfortable with that?
I don't want to go. I just wanted reassurance that I was okay.
That's fine. We can monitor from here if you're okay with staying.
Ashley is a 26-year-old woman with chest pain due to a panic attack. She is alert and oriented, with a history of anxiety and bipolar disorder.
On methadone. EKG showed sinus tach with nonspecific abnormalities, but otherwise normal with no cardiac compromise.
She refused transport but has stable vitals and is in good mental status.
No shortness of breath, abdominal pain, or neurological symptoms reported. She is aox4 and in semi-Fowler's position.
Vitals remain within safe range: heart rate 122, BP 126/81, respirations 18, and oxygen saturation 99%.
No signs of shortness of breath or work of breathing.
No evidence of abdominal pain or discomfort found.
Again, can you tell me where you are and what the situation was when you called?
I'm at home in [CITY] County. I called because I didn't know if I was having a heart attack or anxiety.
Another 12-lead EKG has been completed.
Results remain the same: sinus tach with nonspecific ST and T wave changes.
Respiratory rate still 18, no increase in distress noted.
Can you continue to tell me what you were doing today to show your memory retention?
I've been home all day with my baby. She's six weeks old and is my third child.
Skin remains the same: pink, warm, and dry.
Tympanic temperature is stable at 99.1°F.

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: What led up to the chest pain? Was there an activity or emotion involved? 82. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: events leading up to the event; Patient: I was just sitting with my daughter. It felt sudden but probably from my chronic stress.
How long was the discomfort present before you felt better?
Maybe around two hours. It's not gone completely but has lessened.
You mentioned feeling anxious. Is this a common experience?
Yes. I have a history of postpartum depression with my first two children as well.
Ashley, can you lie back slightly more comfortable so we can check your back?
Sure, moving is fine. I just feel okay here.
Positioning is good, no discomfort indicated from moving.
Let me check your breath sounds again on both sides and your heart.
Bilateral breath sounds heard; no adventitious sounds. Heart rhythm regular.
Any problems sitting or moving your legs or arms?
No, I can move each extremity freely.
Rechecking heart rate - remains steady at 122 beats per minute.
Vitals repeated: Pulse 122, BP 126/81, RR 18, SpO2 99%.
Ashley, would you like to take any medication or need further intervention?
No, I just need more reassurance.
